title: Angelica venenosa
taxonomy: Angelica venenosa is classified within the plant family Apiaceae. This plant is native to the Americas and is also known as American angelica. It is further classified within the genus Angelica and the species A. venenosa. The classification is as follows: kingdom: Plantae, clade: angiosperms, clade: Eudicots, clade: apsidiales, family: Apiaceae, genus: Angelica, species: A. venenosa. The classification of A. venenosa reflects its botanical relationships and characteristics within the plant kingdom.
uses: Angelica venenosa, also known as Eastern Angelica or Eastern Coyote Tobacco, has been used in traditional medicine for various purposes. The plant's roots and leaves are rich in bioactive compounds, which have been traditionally used to treat various health issues. Its medicinal uses include treating fever, rheumatism, and respiratory problems, such as coughs and colds. However, more research is needed to fully understand the plant's efficacy and potential side effects. Further studies are required to confirm its safety and effectiveness for human consumption.
benefits: Angelica venenosa is said to have various health benefits, particularly in traditional medicine. Its roots and leaves are used to treat various ailments, including fever, rheumatism, and respiratory issues. Some studies suggest that Angelica venenosa may have anti-inflammatory properties, which can help reduce pain and swelling. However, more research is needed to fully understand the potential health benefits of Angelica venenosa.

The roots of Angelica venenosa contain compounds with potential anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. side_effects: Angelica venenosa is known to be highly toxic and can cause severe side effects if ingested. The plant contains sesquiterpene lactones, which are toxic compounds that can cause symptoms such as nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Ingestion of the plant can also lead to more severe symptoms, including respiratory failure, cardiac arrest, and even death. Handling the plant can also cause skin irritation and allergic reactions in some individuals. Due to its toxic properties, it is essential to handle Angelica venenosa with caution and avoid ingesting it altogether.
